# UC-19.1.40 · Proxmox Storage Replication (pvesr) Sync Failure and Lag

> **Criticality:** High &middot; **Difficulty:** Advanced &middot; **Pillar:** Observability &middot; **Type:** Availability, Reliability &middot; **Status:** Verified

*We check whether the copies of your virtual machines that should land on a second Proxmox server are actually arriving on time, or have stopped and gone quiet. If that background copy lags, your safety net is thinner than the team thought when they last ran a fire drill, so we raise it before a real site problem meets an empty standby disk.*

---

## Description

Detects Proxmox **pvesr** replication jobs that are falling behind, failing outright, or taking longer than a rolling per-job duration baseline, by consuming JSON from the `GET /api2/json/cluster/replication` (or per-node) REST family with defensive `coalesce()` for `id`, `guest`, `source`/`source_node`, `target`/`target_node`, `error`/`last_error`, `fail_count`/`failcount`, `state`/`status`, and `last_sync` variants, converting `last_sync` from either numeric epoch or ISO-8601 strings to `last_sync_epoch`, computing `lag_sec` and `lag_mult` with a `lookup` to `proxmox_pvesr_intervals` (default 60 minutes), classifying a four-bucket `severity_bucket` and `sev_order`, and optionally correlating a guest with two or more `critical` jobs in a window. The secondary `sourcetype=proxmox:replication:log` is supported for the same eval chain once props normalise the same field names, but the API poll remains the analytical source of truth for schedule-relative lag.

## Value

Replication is how many Proxmox estates credibly promise an off-box copy for a rack loss or a stretched-LAN event without buying Ceph capacity for every guest. When **pvesr** stalls, the organisation quietly loses recovery-point objectives: leadership still believes a DR drill will boot on the B side, SRE believes HA evacuations will have a current disk, and finance has already decided against a second array purchase because “replication covers it.” The business value is to surface RPO slip, repeated attempt counters, and slow sends before a real outage, so capacity, network, and guest owners can act while the data path is repairable, and so audit evidence of DR readiness is tied to the same lag math your operators already defend in the Proxmox UI.

## Implementation

Create `proxmox_pvesr_poll` (or an internal name) on a host with HTTPS to the PVE API, add `pveum` token and `PVEAuditor` mapping, map `proxmox:replication` into `index=virtualization`, deploy `proxmox_pvesr_intervals` plus `local/props` and `local/transforms` for the lookup, schedule the `spl` as a 5- or 15-minute saved search, throttle on `job_id`, and back the alert with a three-row dashboard: worst severity, per-job table, and history chart.

## Detailed Implementation

### Prerequisites
- A Proxmox VE 7.x or 8.x cluster with **pvesr** (Proxmox VE Storage Replication) jobs for guests whose disks you replicate on a schedule between physical nodes, typically over a dedicated storage or management network, without relying on a shared Ceph data plane for those disks. The polling contract below reads the same REST data you would see in the API viewer for /cluster/replication and, after normalisation, matches what operators expect from the CLI. Pull five live JSON objects per site before you freeze field names: some builds emit last_sync as epoch seconds and others as ISO-8601 text, and a few include empty error text while state still flips to ERROR.
- Splunk Universal Forwarder 9.0+ (or a supported heavy forwarder) on a host that can reach the Proxmox HTTPS API, or a member node when policy allows. Search-time roles need access to the index you target (illustrated as `index=virtualization`). The poll interval should be no larger than one half of your fastest pvesr schedule if you need sub-interval lag visibility.
- A `lookups/proxmox_pvesr_intervals.csv` (minimum columns `job_id`, `interval_min`) that stores each job’s expected cadence in minutes for lag multiple calculations. A missing job falls back to 60 minutes in the SPL, which is a deliberate conservative default, not a substitute for a measured schedule.
- Optional side-channel: **ZFS send/recv** performance problems often show in duration growth before a hard `ERROR`— which is why the query keeps per-job `streamstats` on `duration`. You will validate both API truth and, when you enable the optional log path, the raw **zfs receive** strings that appear in **Datacenter → Replication** task views.

**Step 1 — Configure data collection**

On a PVE node with `pveum`, build a read-only user and an API token the Splunk poller can use without a password prompt, then test `pvesh` before you wire the scripted input. Example (interactive password steps omitted):

```text
pveum user add splunk@pve
pveum aclmod / -user splunk@pve -role PVEAuditor
pveum user token add splunk@pve splunk-poll --privsep 0
```

Point `PVE_TOKEN_FILE` at a 0600 file containing the secret issued once by `pveum user token add`.

Place the poller in `$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/proxmox_pvesr_poll/bin/pve_replication_json.py` (mode 0755):

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# Poll Proxmox cluster replication; emits one minified JSON line per job.
import json, os, sys, time, urllib.request
API = os.environ.get("PVE_API", "https://127.0.0.1:8006")
TOKEN_ID = os.environ.get("PVE_TOKEN_ID", "splunk@pve!splunk-poll")
TOKEN_PATH = os.environ.get("PVE_TOKEN_FILE", "/root/.splunk_pve_token")
def fetch(path: str) -> dict:
    url = API.rstrip("/") + path
    sec = open(TOKEN_PATH).read().strip()
    req = urllib.request.Request(
        url,
        headers={"Authorization": f"PVEAPIToken {TOKEN_ID}={sec}"},
    )
    with urllib.request.urlopen(req, timeout=25) as r:
        return json.load(r)
def main() -> None:
    doc = fetch("/api2/json/cluster/replication")
    when = int(time.time())
    for row in doc.get("data", []):
        row["_poll_time"] = when
        if "id" in row and row["id"] is not None:
            row["job_id"] = row["id"]
        print(json.dumps(row, separators=(",", ":")))
if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

`local/inputs.conf` (call a wrapper so credentials stay out of the process list where possible):

```ini
[script://./bin/pve_replication_json.sh]
disabled = 0
interval = 300
index = virtualization
sourcetype = proxmox:replication
source = pve_replication_api
```

`local/bin/pve_replication_json.sh` (mode 0755) exports `PVE_API`, `PVE_TOKEN_ID`, and `PVE_TOKEN_FILE`, then `exec`’s the Python poller. Example: `export PVE_API='https://pve01.mgmt.example.com:8006'; export PVE_TOKEN_ID='splunk@pve!splunk-poll'; export PVE_TOKEN_FILE='/root/.splunk_pve_token'; exec /opt/splunkforwarder/etc/apps/proxmox_pvesr_poll/bin/pve_replication_json.py`.

`local/props.conf` for the primary sourcetype:

```ini
[proxmox:replication]
DATETIME_CONFIG = NONE
TRUNCATE = 0
LINE_BREAKER = ([\r\n]+)
SHOULD_LINEMERGE = false
KV_MODE = json
```

If `_time` is not automatic from JSON, add `INDEXED_EXTRACTIONS` in `local/transforms.conf` to stamp `_time` from `_poll_time` as epoch, or set `TIME_FORMAT` in `props` once you have one representative `_raw` line. Register `proxmox_pvesr_intervals` as a `lookup` in `local/transforms.conf` with `max_matches = 1` on `job_id`.

Optional: forward `pvesr` service logs as `sourcetype=proxmox:replication:log` (tail `/var/log/pve/replicate/*` with `imfile` and `rsyslog`, or stream `journalctl -u pvesr.service` through a UF that tags `sourcetype=linux:journald` in a different UC—if you do, restrict this search to the two whitelisted `proxmox:*` types only unless you have props for the third stream). Keep JSON polling authoritative for `last_sync` and `fail_count`.

**Step 2 — Build the search**

The SPL below starts from both `proxmox:replication` and `proxmox:replication:log` so you can add log-derived jobs later without forking the detection. The first `eval` pass applies `coalesce()` for `id`→`job_id`, guest, endpoint, and error fields, flattens `last_sync` to epoch, rolls `streamstats` for duration anomalies, performs the `lookup` to convert schedule minutes to seconds, bins into five minutes, rolls up to one row per job per window, and finally elevates a guest to `critical` if two or more per-guest job rows in the same window were already `critical` (a practical stand-in for every-direction down on dual-target designs).

```spl
index=virtualization (sourcetype="proxmox:replication" OR sourcetype="proxmox:replication:log")
| eval src_kind=case(
    sourcetype="proxmox:replication", "api_json",
    sourcetype="proxmox:replication:log", "pvesr_log",
    1=1, "other"
  )
| eval job_id=coalesce(id, job_id, repl_id, "")
| eval guest=coalesce(guest, vmid, "unknown")
| eval source=coalesce(source, source_node, src, "")
| eval target=coalesce(target, target_node, dst, dest, "")
| eval err_msg=trim(coalesce(error, last_error, err, ""))
| eval fail_count=tonumber(coalesce(fail_count, failcount, 0), 10)
| eval state=upper(trim(coalesce(state, status, job_state, "")))
| eval lsrc=toString(coalesce(last_sync, lastsync, last_sync_epoch, ""))
| eval last_sync_epoch=if(isnum(lsrc) AND lsrc!="", tonumber(lsrc, 10), null())
| eval last_sync_epoch=coalesce(
    last_sync_epoch,
    strptime(lsrc, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z"),
    strptime(lsrc, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"),
    strptime(replace(lsrc, "Z", ""), "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S"),
    strptime(lsrc, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S"),
    0
  )
| eval duration=tonumber(coalesce(duration, last_duration, sync_duration, 0), 10)
| sort 0 + _time
| streamstats current=f window=10 global=f avg(duration) as dur_avg stdev(duration) as dur_std by job_id
| eval dur_breach=if(dur_std>0 AND isnotnull(dur_std) AND duration>(dur_avg+3*dur_std), 1, 0)
| lookup proxmox_pvesr_intervals job_id OUTPUT interval_min
| eval interval_min=tonumber(coalesce(interval_min, 60), 10)
| eval interval_sec=if(interval_min>0, interval_min*60, 3600)
| eval lag_sec=if(last_sync_epoch>0, now()-last_sync_epoch, 999999999)
| eval lag_mult=if(interval_sec>0, lag_sec/interval_sec, 0)
| eval sev0=case(
    (state="ERROR" OR len(err_msg)>0) AND (fail_count>=3 OR lag_mult>=4), "critical",
    state="ERROR" OR len(err_msg)>0 OR fail_count>=1 OR lag_mult>=2, "warning",
    state="WARNING" OR lag_mult>=1.5 OR dur_breach=1, "watch",
    1=1, "ok"
  )
| bin _time span=5m aligntime=earliest
| stats
    latest(src_kind) as src_kind
    latest(guest) as guest
    latest(err_msg) as err_msg
    max(fail_count) as fail_count
    max(last_sync_epoch) as last_sync_epoch
    max(lag_sec) as lag_sec
    max(lag_mult) as lag_mult
    max(dur_breach) as dur_breach
    max(duration) as duration
    latest(state) as state
    latest(sev0) as sev_pre
  by _time, job_id, source, target
| eval severity_bucket=sev_pre
| eventstats sum(eval(if(severity_bucket="critical",1,0))) as crit_sum by guest
| eval dual_direction_critical=if(crit_sum>=2,1,0)
| eval severity_bucket=if(dual_direction_critical=1,"critical",severity_bucket)
| eval sev_order=case(severity_bucket="critical",1, severity_bucket="warning",2, severity_bucket="watch",3, 1=1,4)
| where sev_order<=3
| table _time, job_id, guest, source, target, state, err_msg, fail_count, last_sync_epoch, lag_sec, lag_mult, duration, dur_breach, src_kind, dual_direction_critical, crit_sum, severity_bucket, sev_order
| sort 0 sev_order, -lag_mult
```

Narrative: `src_kind` is only an audit aid for mixed ingestion. The `sev0` `case()` encodes a four-bucket `severity_bucket` with critical tied to the worst combinations of `ERROR`, `fail_count`, and `lag_mult` relative to the lookup, warning for first failures or doubled lag, and watch for the API warning state, intermediate lag, or a duration spike above rolling mean + three standard deviations. The closing `where sev_order<=3` is appropriate for a dashboard card that should stay quiet in the green. Remove that stanza in ad-hoc exploration when you are proving parity with `pvesr list` on the command line.

**Step 3 — Validate the results**

On the cluster, run `pvesh get /cluster/replication --output-format json | jq`. Pick one `id` such as 105-0. In Splunk, `index=virtualization sourcetype=proxmox:replication job_id="105-0" OR 105-0` (subject to how `KV_MODE` names `id`) and expand `last_sync_epoch` after the eval block—compare the seconds to the Proxmox UI at **Datacenter → Replication** for guest 105, which should align within one poll and any manual clock offset you accept between node and search head. If you have enabled the file or journal secondary, verify that a forced failure (`pvesr disable 105-0` in a lab) produces non-empty `err_msg` or a move to `ERROR` on the next schedule, and that the alert recovers with `pvesr enable` plus a full successful run.

**Step 4 — Operationalize as alert + dashboard**

For schedules where pvesr runs every fifteen minutes, run the saved search and alert on a five-minute `cron` with a lookback of at least `45m@m` so three missed ticks can surface. For hourly schedules, a fifteen-minute `cron` with a two-hour lookback is usually sufficient, but keep the alert window a multiple of the largest `interval_min` in your `lookup` table. Throttle on `job_id` for thirty to sixty minutes to absorb single-link brownouts. The dashboard: single value of jobs in `critical`, sortable table of `job_id` / `guest` / `source` / `target` / `lag_mult` / `fail_count` / `severity_bucket`, and a timechart of `sev_order` (stacked) or a sync-success proxy (`if(sev_order=4,1,0)`) in a development clone. Wire alert actions to include direct links to the Proxmox task history for the job and to your runbook for storage replication, not a generic landing page.

**Step 5 — Troubleshoot common issues**

1) API token is correct but the poll returns 401: confirm `PVE_TOKEN_ID` includes `user!tokenid` exactly, and the secret file has no extra newline. 2) `last_sync` is ISO-only on your build and Splunk still shows `0`: add a `strptime` format your samples need (include fractional seconds if present). 3) Every job is `ERROR` at once: check ZFS free space, pool I/O wait, and whether the full cluster just lost corosync quorum—that can stall pvesr even when guests still ping. 4) `WARNING` without actionable error text: often snapshot overlap while the pool reconciles; wait one interval before a bridge if duration is flat. 5) `fail_count` not clearing after success: treat as a counter until service restart; chart deltas with `delta fail_count` to see real regression. 6) `lookup` not matching: job ids from API include forms like 100-0 while CSV used 100-0 with spaces—constrain CSV formatting. 7) Journal ship stops after a `pve-manager` upgrade: confirm `pvesr.timer` and `pvesr.service` still emit under `journalctl -u` on the node you collect from. 8) LXC vs VM: container ids under 200 in some naming schemes confuse CMDB joins—carry `guest` straight from the API instead of a fragile inventory guess. 9) The bin window is a quarter of your schedule: for very slow jobs, widen the `span` in `bin` to match half the schedule, not a fixed five minutes. 10) Dual-direction flag is noisy on single-site DR with only one target: you can set `eval dual_direction_critical=0` in a local macro when not applicable. 11) Polling only a member that is offline: add a second poller on another node, or use a management VIP that always lands on a live member. 12) The duration anomaly is hot during guest-side snapshot flattening: read the **Datacenter → Replication** task details before you blame hardware.


## SPL

```spl
index=virtualization (sourcetype="proxmox:replication" OR sourcetype="proxmox:replication:log")
| eval src_kind=case(
    sourcetype="proxmox:replication", "api_json",
    sourcetype="proxmox:replication:log", "pvesr_log",
    1=1, "other"
  )
| eval job_id=coalesce(id, job_id, repl_id, "")
| eval guest=coalesce(guest, vmid, "unknown")
| eval source=coalesce(source, source_node, src, "")
| eval target=coalesce(target, target_node, dst, dest, "")
| eval err_msg=trim(coalesce(error, last_error, err, ""))
| eval fail_count=tonumber(coalesce(fail_count, failcount, 0), 10)
| eval state=upper(trim(coalesce(state, status, job_state, "")))
| eval lsrc=toString(coalesce(last_sync, lastsync, last_sync_epoch, ""))
| eval last_sync_epoch=if(isnum(lsrc) AND lsrc!="", tonumber(lsrc, 10), null())
| eval last_sync_epoch=coalesce(
    last_sync_epoch,
    strptime(lsrc, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z"),
    strptime(lsrc, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"),
    strptime(replace(lsrc, "Z", ""), "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S"),
    strptime(lsrc, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S"),
    0
  )
| eval duration=tonumber(coalesce(duration, last_duration, sync_duration, 0), 10)
| sort 0 + _time
| streamstats current=f window=10 global=f avg(duration) as dur_avg stdev(duration) as dur_std by job_id
| eval dur_breach=if(dur_std>0 AND isnotnull(dur_std) AND duration>(dur_avg+3*dur_std), 1, 0)
| lookup proxmox_pvesr_intervals job_id OUTPUT interval_min
| eval interval_min=tonumber(coalesce(interval_min, 60), 10)
| eval interval_sec=if(interval_min>0, interval_min*60, 3600)
| eval lag_sec=if(last_sync_epoch>0, now()-last_sync_epoch, 999999999)
| eval lag_mult=if(interval_sec>0, lag_sec/interval_sec, 0)
| eval sev0=case(
    (state="ERROR" OR len(err_msg)>0) AND (fail_count>=3 OR lag_mult>=4), "critical",
    state="ERROR" OR len(err_msg)>0 OR fail_count>=1 OR lag_mult>=2, "warning",
    state="WARNING" OR lag_mult>=1.5 OR dur_breach=1, "watch",
    1=1, "ok"
  )
| bin _time span=5m aligntime=earliest
| stats
    latest(src_kind) as src_kind
    latest(guest) as guest
    latest(err_msg) as err_msg
    max(fail_count) as fail_count
    max(last_sync_epoch) as last_sync_epoch
    max(lag_sec) as lag_sec
    max(lag_mult) as lag_mult
    max(dur_breach) as dur_breach
    max(duration) as duration
    latest(state) as state
    latest(sev0) as sev_pre
  by _time, job_id, source, target
| eval severity_bucket=sev_pre
| eventstats sum(eval(if(severity_bucket="critical",1,0))) as crit_sum by guest
| eval dual_direction_critical=if(crit_sum>=2,1,0)
| eval severity_bucket=if(dual_direction_critical=1,"critical",severity_bucket)
| eval sev_order=case(severity_bucket="critical",1, severity_bucket="warning",2, severity_bucket="watch",3, 1=1,4)
| where sev_order<=3
| table _time, job_id, guest, source, target, state, err_msg, fail_count, last_sync_epoch, lag_sec, lag_mult, duration, dur_breach, src_kind, dual_direction_critical, crit_sum, severity_bucket, sev_order
| sort 0 sev_order, -lag_mult
```

## Visualization

(1) Single value: count of `severity_bucket="critical"` in the time picker. (2) Table: `job_id`, `guest`, `source`→`target`, `lag_sec`, `lag_mult`, `fail_count`, `err_msg`, `severity_bucket`, `dual_direction_critical`, sorted by `sev_order` then `-lag_mult`. (3) Timechart: per `job_id` `max(sev_order)` to show when a job oscillates between watch and warning. (4) Optional sparkline: `streamstats` clone on raw before `bin` to plot `duration` with static thresholds from the lookup. (5) Drill: base search filtered to one `job_id` across both whitelisted `proxmox:*` sourcetypes.

## Known False Positives

•

- Rolling node OS or Proxmox minor upgrades where the source or target is intentionally fenced for thirty to ninety minutes: **pvesr** may show `ERROR` until the node returns; use a `maintenance_windows` lookup on `source` and `target` FQDNs and suppress only when a change is recorded.
•
- A planned `pvesr disable` before disk slot replacement or a RAM increase on a single member: the job will sit `ERROR` with a documented, expected string until re-enabled; page only when the MOP time elapses without `enable`.
•
- A transient `connection reset` on the storage vLAN while you re-tag switch ports: one interval of `ERROR` is normal; require two windows or a second sourcetype echo before a hardware bridge.
•
- A lab `zfs create` or `zpool` scrub that fills the pool to prove alerting: the resulting `ERROR` is the whole point, not a production fault.
•
- A deliberate `pveceph stop` on a cluster that also runs some guests from Ceph-backed disks: pvesr jobs for those objects may show ERROR until the mon/osd path returns, even if unrelated guests still run—triage with the Ceph UC, not a disk RMA for pvesr alone.
•
- Heavy snapshot consolidation in the guest that stretches `duration` but leaves `state=OK` and empty `err_msg`—`dur_breach` in watch is expected; ignore if only watch-tier fires and UI tasks show only slow progress.
•
- A DR test where you pause replication in both directions in script order: the dual-direction flags may trip even though the business audience is watching; annotate the Pager with `DR-TEST-2026` in the ticket title.
•
- Intentional `pvesr disable` on one direction while you resize a qcow2 target on a second storage path: a single `ERROR` row is the desired visibility, not a dual-site fire drill.
•
- A maintenance window for cluster network renumbering with documented brief loss of the API on one member: the poller may miss one JSON tick; widen inputs `interval` slightly rather than re-page on a single null poll.
•
- A container id below 200 that your CMDB still labels as a VM, making inventory links look `unknown` in dashboards while the pvesr data is still correct; fix metadata, not the search.
•
- A storage-only bench where you intentionally starve the replication NIC to prove `lag_mult` math: the alert is a validation success, not a false alarm, but it should be filtered from production on-call with an index=lab rule.
•
- A short `WARNING` while **zfs receive** re-checks a snapshot after you cleared a name collision manually in the UI; if `fail_count` is zero and the next run is clean, do not re-open a resolved bridge.

- [Proxmox VE pveum — user and token management](https://pve.proxmox.com/pve-docs/pveum.1.html)
- [OpenZFS — zfs(8) manual (send and receive)](https://man.openzfs.org/8/zfs#send_and_receive)
